retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Crossbite -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Anterior Crossbite
retrieved_docs.content: The type of movement required for correction is assessed. Removable appliances can be considered for tipping movement; the fixed appliance is indicated for bodily tooth movement. The appliance should incorporate these features and good anterior retention to counteract the displaying effect of the active element. A bite plane or an active component could move the teeth to free the occlusion with the opposing arch. Fixed appliances can be indicated when there is insufficient overbite to retain the corrected incisors. Open coil springs can be used in straight wire mechanics to create enough arch length to position the teeth. A negative root torque is sometimes required for palatally placed upper incisors. An adequate overbite and a normal inclination of the long axis of the tooth to be treated are important for retention stability.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Clinical Significance -- Vertical Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The angular measurement of the maxilla is determined by measuring the angle from the incisor to nasion-A, while the angular measurement of the mandible is calculated from the incisor to nasion-B. These values indicate the inclination of teeth—proclined teeth are angled forward, retroclined teeth are tilted backward, and normally inclined teeth are in a neutral position. [8] The normal incisor to nasion-A angle is 22 degrees, and the normal incisor to nasion-B angle is 25 degrees. An increased incisor to nasion-A or nasion-B angle indicates proclination of the incisor, while a decreased angle suggests retroclination. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Clinical Significance -- Vertical Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The position of mandibular incisors is further evaluated by the angle formed between the long axis of the tooth and the mandibular plane, which extends from gonion to gnathion. [10] The normal mandibular incisor to mandibular plane angle (Go-Gn) is 87 degrees. An increased mandibular incisor to mandibular plane angle indicates that the incisors are proclined, whereas a decreased value indicates the incisors are retroclined. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Clinical Significance -- Incisor Position: Linear Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The linear measurement of the maxilla is taken from the maxillary incisor to nasion-A, while the linear measurement of the mandible is taken from the mandibular incisor to nasion-B. These measurements describe the relationship between the tooth and its supporting basal bone, indicating whether the tooth is in a normal, procumbent (ahead of the supporting bone), or recumbent (behind the supporting bone) position. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Root Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: If the coronal fragment is not displaced, no treatment is required. If the coronal fragment is displaced and is not excessively mobile, leave the coronal fragment to reposition spontaneously, even if occlusal interference is present. A clinical follow-up examination is recommended after one week, six to eight weeks, and one year. Continue clinical follow-up yearly until the eruption of the permanent successor.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Clinical Significance -- Incisor Position: Linear Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The average value for both incisor to nasion-A and incisor to nasion-B is 4 mm. An increased incisor to nasion-A or -B value indicates that the incisor is procumbent, whereas a decreased value suggests that the incisor is recumbent. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Crossbite -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Posterior Crossbite
retrieved_docs.content: Fixed orthodontic appliances can be used to correct posterior crossbites, as they provide 3-dimensional control over the tooth. The arches can be kept slightly expanded or constricted, depending upon the movement required. Cross-elastics of 3/16-inch diameter exerting a force of 2.5 to 4.5 oz can correct individual tooth crossbites in the posterior segment.

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Crossbite -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Posterior Crossbite
retrieved_docs.content: The coffin spring is an omega-shaped wire appliance that corrects crossbite in young, developing dentition. The expansion produced is slow and bilaterally symmetrical.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Teeth -- Equipment -- Forceps
retrieved_docs.content: Forceps are used to apply apical pressure onto a tooth with either a rotational or buccolingual movement or a combination of both. Correct selection of forceps for the tooth being removed is key to success in using them, and a practitioner should be aware of the variations between forceps for anterior teeth, premolars, molars, maxillary third molars, roots, and cowhorns.

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Crossbite -- Treatment / Management -- Preadolescents
retrieved_docs.content: Use of tongue blade: This method can correct the development of crossbite. A tongue blade resembles a flat ice cream stick placed inside the mouth, contacting the erupting tooth in a crossbite on its palatal side. During the slight closure of the jaws, the opposing side of the tooth comes in contact with the labial aspect of the opposing mandibular tooth. Light forces generated during this period might help the tooth attain a better position. Catalan’s appliance or lower inclined plane: The lower inclined plane is constructed at an angle of 45 to the maxillary occlusal plane and can be cemented on lower incisors. Face masks and Rapid maxillary expansion: This method can be used when a skeletal-transverse deficiency occurs in the maxilla Frankel III appliance: Can be used to correct a developing Class III skeletal malocclusion. Chin cup appliance: It can be used to redirect the growth of a prognathic mandible.

retrieved_docs.title: Excessive Gingival Display -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior dentoalveolar extrusion is the overeruption of the maxillary incisors, resulting in excessive gingival display due to a more coronal position of the gingival margins. [1] Tooth wear and an anterior deep bite may be the causes of the anterior extrusion. A compensatory incisor overeruption is seen in cases of anterior tooth wear. [1] A discrepancy between the anterior and posterior occlusal planes is usually found in deep bite cases. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Dens invaginatus should be evaluated radiographically with either a series of periapical radiographs from different angles or a CBCT to evaluate the internal structures. Depending on the complexity of the tooth's internal structure or the extent of the invagination, treatment and management strategies of these teeth can range from monitoring to surgical intervention. Treatment has phased into an endodontic approach compared to the previous oral surgery-driven approach with extractions due to the development of better diagnostic aids, such as the CBCT. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Crossbite -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Anterior Crossbite
retrieved_docs.content: The presence or absence of anterior displacement from centric relation to centric occlusion during mandibular closure must be established as a part of the diagnosis. The distinction between true class III and pseudo-class III malocclusions impacts treatment plans, prognosis, and stability. [6] Factors to be considered for treatment: What type of movement is required Overbite at the end of treatment Extraction or nonextraction If the movement of the opposing tooth required

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Crossbite -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Posterior Crossbite
retrieved_docs.content: NiTi Expanders: These nickel-titanium wire shapes can be attached to a lingual sheath welded to molar bands cemented to the maxillary first permanent molars. Various sizes are available and must be selected depending on the amount of expansion desired and the pretreatment width of the palate.

retrieved_docs.title: Excessive Gingival Display -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Dentoalveolar or orthognathic surgery becomes the only alternative in some severe cases of vertical maxillary excess: LeFort osteotomy, or maxillo-mandibular reposition, combining LeFort and Obwegeser mandibular osteotomy and surgical orthodontic treatment. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Technique or Treatment -- Standardized Lateral Cephalometric Radiographs
retrieved_docs.content: A standardized technique is essential for obtaining radiographs to ensure accurate comparisons over time and between patients. Image quality depends heavily on the proper positioning of the patient. The patient should be positioned with the Frankfort plane horizontal, ear rests placed in the external auditory meatuses, the nasion aligned on the bridge of the nose, and teeth in centric occlusion. [6] The radiographic source is fixed at a distance of 5 feet (150–180 cm) from the patient's mid-sagittal plane, with a film-to-mid-sagittal plane distance of 30 cm. [6] A calibrated steel ruler is included in each image, ensuring precise measurements are documented.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1226
retrieved_docs.content: these specific presentations, creating a hierarchical structure that integrates Face or brow presentation, Prolapsed arm of fetus, Transverse or oblique presentation, and Unspecified malposition or malpresentation under a unified framework. This integrated representation supports clinicians in identifying, categorizing, and managing various abnormal fetal presentations to optimize maternal‑fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 961
retrieved_docs.content: labor and delivery. Recognizing these interrelated conditions enables clinicians to anticipate potential delivery challenges, plan appropriate interventions (such as external cephalic version, cervical assessment, or operative delivery), and optimize outcomes for both mother and child.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal and fetal conditions that influence labor and delivery encompass a broad spectrum of obstetric complications, uterine functional abnormalities, cervical pathology, multiple‑gestation pregnancies, fetal size disproportion, abnormal fetal presentations, and drug contraindications in pregnancy. Abnormal fetal presentations—breech malpresentation, face or brow presentation, mentum (chin‑first) presentation, transverse or oblique lie, arm presentation (سوء المجيء الجنيني, تدلّي ذراع الجنين), shoulder presentation (وضعية المَجيء الكتفي), funic presentation, and other malpositions or unstable fetal positions—can impede vaginal descent and often require operative delivery or specialized obstetric management; they are linked to prolonged first‑stage or second‑stage labor, failure to induce labor, uterine hypotonia (رحم ناقص التوتر), secondary uterine inertia (عطالة رحمية ثانوية), and prolapse of the fetal arm. Anterior and posterior asynclitism represent specific uterine functional

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1697
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior asynclitism and posterior asynclitism are distinct subtypes of uterine function, each describing a specific asynclitic presentation of the fetal head during labor. Both conditions are considered child concepts within the broader category of uterine function, reflecting variations in fetal head alignment that can influence the mechanics of childbirth and guide obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2314
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition; malpresentation is classified as a protracted (prolapsed) arm of the fetus, with the episode of care unspecified or not applicable.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2026
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal and fetal conditions affecting labor and delivery is a broad category that includes several specific obstetric problems. Among these, breech malpresentation successfully converted to cephalic presentation represents a situation in which a fetus initially in a breech position is turned to the head‑first (cephalic) position before birth, thereby improving the prospects for a vaginal delivery. Cicatrix of cervix affecting pregnancy refers to a cervical scar or fibrous tissue that can compromise cervical integrity and potentially interfere with the progress of pregnancy and the mechanics of labor. Tumor of cervix affecting pregnancy denotes the presence of a cervical neoplasm that may pose risks to both maternal health and fetal development, often necessitating careful monitoring and tailored delivery planning. Additionally, سوء تموضع الجنين (fetal malposition) describes abnormal positioning of the fetus, such as breech or transverse lie, which can impede normal labor progression and may require interventions such as external cephalic version, operative delivery, or cesarean section. Together, these conditions illustrate the diverse ways in which maternal cervical pathology and fetal positioning can impact labor and delivery, underscoring the importance of accurate diagnosis and individualized management to optimize outcomes for both mother and child.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 731
retrieved_docs.content: NORMAL DELIVERY, AND OTHER INDICATIONS FOR CARE IN PREGNANCY, LABOR, AND DELIVERY, indicating that understanding malpositions informs decisions about normal versus assisted delivery. Finally, Mentum presentation of fetus is identified as an isa relationship with مّجيء وَجْهي (face presentation), specifying that a mentum (chin) presentation is a particular form of facial fetal presentation. Together, these interrelated concepts illustrate how various fetal positions, malpresentations, and physiological reproductive events are organized and interpreted to guide obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: Malposition; malpresentation are specific manifestations of fetal position instability (عدم استقرار وضعية الجنين). Both terms describe abnormal fetal positioning that can compromise the progress of labor and increase the risk of delivery complications. Recognizing that malposition and malpresentation are subcategories of fetal position instability helps clinicians anticipate potential problems, monitor the pregnancy more closely, and plan appropriate obstetric interventions to ensure safer outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2393
retrieved_docs.content: External obstetrical version is a fetal procedure that is classified among other delivery procedures, indicating that it is a type of intervention performed on the fetus to reposition it prior to birth and is grouped within the broader category of delivery methods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: Face OR brow presentation of fetus is a specific fetal presentation that has as its parent the broader category Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable. This broader category is classified as a Malposition; malpresentation, indicating that the fetus is positioned abnormally in the birth canal. Understanding that both Face OR brow presentation of fetus and the unspecified Face or brow presentation fall under the umbrella of malposition or malpresentation informs clinicians that these presentations may complicate labor and delivery, prompting careful obstetric assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 109
retrieved_docs.content: disorders, and obstetric interventions together form a comprehensive framework for risk assessment, care planning, and management of maternal‑fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: فالج is a general medical condition, and فالج طفلي represents a more specific, narrower form of فالج, indicating the infantile variant of this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 994
retrieved_docs.content: The fetal presentation entities “Face OR brow presentation of fetus” and “Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus” are each linked to more generic descriptors—“Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” respectively—indicating that the specific presentations are sub‑categories of broader, episode‑agnostic classifications. Both of these broader presentation categories are classified under the overarching obstetric condition “Malposition; malpresentation,” which groups together abnormal fetal positions that deviate from the normal cephalic presentation. “Malposition; malpresentation” itself is identified as an indication for obstetric management within the domain “Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery,” highlighting that any malpresentation warrants specific clinical attention during prenatal care, labor, and delivery planning. Within the “Malposition; malpresentation” group, two additional sub‑types are recognized: “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” and “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” The prolapsed‑arm condition also has a parent term expressed in Arabic, “تدلّي ذراع الجنين,” which serves as the linguistic equivalent of the English descriptor. Overall, the hierarchy demonstrates that specific fetal malpresentations—face or brow presentation, transverse or oblique presentation, and prolapsed fetal arm—are organized under the general category of malposition/malpresentation, which is a key indication for specialized obstetric care throughout pregnancy, labor, and delivery. This structured classification aids clinicians in recognizing, documenting, and managing abnormal fetal positions to optimize maternal‑fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 958
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Malposition; malpresentation” serves as a hierarchical classification for several abnormal fetal presentations. Within this group, “Face or brow presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” is classified as a malposition/malpresentation and functions as the parent term for the specific entity “Face OR brow presentation of fetus.” Likewise, “Transverse or oblique presentation, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” is also classified as a malposition/malpresentation and acts as the parent term for the specific entity “Transverse OR oblique presentation of fetus.” In addition to these presentations, the malposition/malpresentation category includes “Prolapsed arm of fetus, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable.” Together, these relationships delineate a structured taxonomy of fetal malpositions that is essential for obstetric diagnosis, risk assessment, and the planning of appropriate intrapartum management.
